metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how Verified=MAX is achieved for every non-tip member of the combined chain (e.g., bot casts Verified +1 on each member from the single combined-tree run, with required ACL grant and gate-integrity rationale per ADR-0040 R5).
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how the Verified gate is satisfied for non-tip chain members after assembly. Under the existing `copyCondition = changekind:NO_CODE_CHANGE` (project.config line 152 / ADR-0040), rebasing any change onto the combined chain mints a TRIVIAL_REBASE or larger patchset — which drops the Verified vote. 'Triggers exactly ONE CI run on the combined tip' means only the tip change receives a new Verified signal; every non-tip member is left with Verified=0, failing the submit-requirement `label:Verified=MAX`. The plan does not describe how the bot casts Verified on non-tip members, or how the gate integrity holds — making the core mechanism mechanically incomplete as written.
findings.location: AC2 ('Triggers exactly ONE CI run on the combined tip') and AC3 ('On green, submits the whole batch atomically')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'The bot casts Verified +1 on every member of the combined chain (not just the tip) using the single combined-tree CI result; the vote-per-member ACL is pre-authorized; the gate-integrity argument (each member's diff was tested as part of the combined tree) is recorded in an ADR amendment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly handle the FFO post-CI submit race: specify what happens when the combined-chain submit is rejected because main advanced during CI (e.g., discard batch, re-assemble on new tip, re-run CI, or fall back to Phase 1 serial landing for this batch).
findings.finding: The plan's 'ancestor-atomic submit' mechanism has a hidden FFO TOCTOU race: under Fast Forward Only (project.config `action = fast forward only`, ADR-0040), the entire combined chain becomes non-submittable the instant any other change lands on `main` between CI completion and the submit call. The plan presents atomic submit as a terminal step with no acknowledgement of this race, no retry loop, and no fallback — yet this race is the dominant failure mode under any non-trivial landing cadence (ADR-0040 §Consequences: 'median inter-landing gap ≈ CI duration; ~46% of landings overlap a prior CI run').
findings.location: AC3 ('On green, submits the whole batch atomically (ancestor-atomic submit); the tested tip == the landed tree')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/AC: 'If main advances between CI completion and submit, the batch is discarded and each stack is returned to the Autosubmit queue for the next batch cycle (or Phase 1 serial fallback is invoked), rather than attempting a rebase-and-resubmit of the full batch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g., `gh run list --workflow=ci.yml --limit=1 | awk '{print $1}'` to assert exactly one run for AC2; `git rev-parse HEAD` == `gh api /repos/.../pulls/{pr}/head` for AC3's SHA check).
findings.finding: No proving commands or implementation steps are described for any of the four ACs; the plan has only a Why/What/Scope/AC structure with zero verification commands, so an executor cannot produce evidence of doneness without inventing the verification approach from scratch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix:
For AC2: `gh run list --workflow=ci.yml --branch=<batch-tip> --json databaseId | jq -e '[.[] | select(.status=="completed")] | length == 1'`
For AC3: assert `git rev-parse <batch-tip>` equals the SHA of the submitted commit on main.
For AC1: `git log --oneline main..<batch-tip> | wc -l` equals the expected stack depth.
For AC4: add a config constant (e.g., `MAX_BATCH_JOBS=N`) and assert it in a unit test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism by which the existing gerrit-to-platform per-patchset-created dispatch is suppressed for intermediate batch members and how a single combined-tip GHA run's Verified conclusion is cast back to all N changes in the batch (or name the alternative CI-trigger API surface used instead).
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 ('Triggers exactly ONE CI run on the combined tip') directly contradicts the existing gerrit-to-platform dispatch model: gerrit-verify.yaml is triggered per patchset-created event, votes on a single GERRIT_CHANGE_NUMBER/GERRIT_PATCHSET_NUMBER, and uses a per-Change-Id concurrency group. A combined N-change batch would normally fire N independent g2p dispatches. No mechanism to suppress per-change g2p events for intermediate chain members, or to fan out a single combined Verified vote to all N changes, is named anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Triggers exactly ONE CI run on the combined tip (not one per change).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Gerrit API surface (endpoint + required ACL) used for cross-uploader chain assembly and confirm it is compatible with the FFO submit type and the submitWholeTopic limitation documented in ADR-0040; add a spike if this has not been verified against a live Gerrit instance.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'assembles queued changes/stacks into ONE combined linear chain on the current main tip' (AC1) but names no Gerrit API endpoint or git operation for cross-stack, cross-uploader relation-chain assembly. Combining independently-uploaded changes (each with its own uploader identity, Change-Id, and possibly distinct topic) into a single Gerrit relation chain is a novel Gerrit operation not previously used in this codebase; its feasibility (especially with the FFO submit type and per-change ACL restrictions in project.config) is unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'Assembles queued changes/stacks into one combined linear chain on the current main tip.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the Graphite/bors analogy with a Gerrit-native precedent (e.g., a citation to Gerrit's own submit-whole-topic behavior, or a documented Gerrit commit-queue bot that performs the same combined-chain submit), or add an explicit spike to verify the pattern works under Gerrit FFO with multi-uploader stacks.
findings.finding: The plan invokes 'Graphite/bors stack-aware model on Gerrit relation chains' as the feasibility precedent for the combined-chain atomic submit, but Graphite and bors are GitHub-PR-native tools with no Gerrit API surface. The analogy is asserted but does not constitute a verified proof that Gerrit's relation-chain submit semantics support the same batching pattern, particularly given that ADR-0040 explicitly documents a Gerrit FFO + submitWholeTopic limitation for parallel sibling changes.
findings.location: What section: 'Graphite/bors stack-aware model on Gerrit relation chains'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the auth and ACL preconditions for Phase 2's new integration surfaces: (a) bot credentials needed to rebase/push changes on behalf of other uploaders, and (b) credentials/API surface used to cast a single combined Verified vote across all N batch members.
findings.finding: Auth/HTTPS preconditions for the new Phase 2 integration surfaces (cross-stack chain assembly requiring bot credentials for each uploader's changes, and the single combined-tip Verified vote cast mechanism) are not stated anywhere in the plan. The plan defers all auth to 'Depends on Phase 1' but Phase 2 introduces new credential requirements (e.g., on_behalf_of_uploader ACL or push access for rebase across uploaders) that Phase 1's auth setup may not cover.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Depends on Phase 1.' — no auth/HTTPS preconditions for Phase 2's new surfaces.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the asserted '~3 concurrent runs' with the actual GHA Free-tier concurrent job limit (or the observed repo-specific limit) and cross-reference the `gerrit-verify.yaml` matrix job count to derive the correct AC4 batch-size bound.
findings.finding: The Why section asserts 'Free-tier GHA concurrency cap (~3 concurrent runs)' as the design constraint driving the batching strategy. The actual `gerrit-verify.yaml` uses a per-change concurrency group (`gerrit-verify-${{ github.event.inputs.GERRIT_CHANGE_ID }}`), not a global cap of 3. GitHub's Free tier allows 20 concurrent jobs (not 3 workflow runs), and no documentation in the repo mentions a cap of 3. The stated cap is ungrounded and may lead to an incorrectly-sized batch bound in AC4.
findings.location: Why section — 'Free-tier GHA concurrency cap (~3 concurrent runs)'; AC4 — 'Batch size bounded to one CI run's job budget (fits the GHA concurrency cap)'
findings.suggested_fix: Measure the actual concurrency ceiling (e.g., via GHA billing settings or a documented per-repo cap) and state the batch size bound as: 'no more than floor(GHA_concurrent_jobs / jobs_per_gerrit-verify_run) stacks per batch', referencing the gerrit-verify.yaml matrix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the Phase 1 story/ticket that provisions the `Autosubmit` Gerrit label and gate Phase 2 on its completion; verify the label appears in `infra/gerrit/project.config` before Phase 2 work begins.
findings.finding: The plan's AC1 pivots on 'queued `Autosubmit` changes/stacks' as the input queue source, implying the `Autosubmit` Gerrit label is an available, deployed artifact. No such label exists in `infra/gerrit/project.config` (which defines only `LLM-Review` and `Verified`), and no other file in the repo defines or references it. The plan says 'Depends on Phase 1' but does not explicitly confirm that Phase 1's delivery of the `Autosubmit` label is the prerequisite, leaving the queue-source mechanism ungrounded.
findings.location: AC1 / What section — 'queued `Autosubmit` changes/stacks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Prerequisite: [Story S-N / ticket ID] must be merged and the `Autosubmit` label live in `infra/gerrit/project.config` before Phase 2 implementation begins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out path for the atomic land (e.g., 'revert the merge commit' or 'reopen constituent changes on a new branch') to satisfy T4's requirement that irreversible steps carry a stated reversibility mechanism.
findings.finding: The atomic submit step (AC3) is irreversible once changes land on main; the plan defers batch-failure handling to a separate story but provides no rollback/back-out path or revert strategy for the case where a defect is discovered after the atomic land, leaving the irreversible operation without an explicit reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: Scope section; Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the What/Scope section a concrete work item for enforcing and documenting the batch-size bound (e.g., a named constant or config field capping the batch, plus a README/inline note), so AC4 has a corresponding described deliverable.
findings.finding: AC4 describes two deliverables — bounding batch size to one CI run's job budget AND documenting that bound — but neither appears as described work in the What or Scope sections, making AC4 an orphan criterion with no corresponding implementation story.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; What section
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'Enforce a configurable MAX_BATCH_SIZE constant (default: N, derived from GHA job budget) and record its derivation in a code comment or README section.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the batch-size bound a named, configurable parameter (e.g., env var or config field) rather than a hardcoded constant, and document both the value and its derivation.
findings.finding: The batch-size threshold is tied to the GHA concurrency cap — a value expected to change (plan upgrades, tier changes, repo growth) — but the plan does not designate it as a configurable parameter; 'documents the bound' implies it may be hardcoded with a comment.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Batch size bounded to one CI run's job budget (fits the GHA concurrency cap); documents the bound.'
findings.suggested_fix: Expose the bound as a named constant or config key (e.g., AUTOSUBMIT_BATCH_MAX_JOBS) with an inline comment referencing the GHA tier and the derivation rationale, so it can be updated without a logic change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error-handling policy for each failure point (chain assembly conflict → abort + re-queue, CI trigger failure → retry with backoff, atomic submit failure → idempotent retry or safe rollback) and document them in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for the three principal failure points: (1) chain assembly fails (e.g., merge conflict between queued stacks), (2) the GHA CI trigger call fails or times out, (3) the atomic submit call fails after CI is green. In all three cases the plan is silent on retry, backoff, or degraded-mode behaviour, meaning errors will be swallowed and state left inconsistent.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria — all ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Failure modes' section enumerating: (a) conflict on assembly → discard batch, return changes to queue individually; (b) GHA trigger failure → exponential-backoff retry up to N times, then alert; (c) submit failure after green → idempotent re-submit (same tree SHA), alert if repeated failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify instrumentation (at minimum structured log events + one alert) for each major state transition: batch assembled, CI triggered, CI result received, submit attempted, submit succeeded/failed.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for any of the new failure points (chain assembly, CI trigger, CI result polling, atomic submit). Operators have no defined metric, log line, or alert to detect a stuck or failed batch run.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full queue item lifecycle for this phase: who removes items on successful land, what state items revert to on batch failure, and how the queue is left safe/consistent in both outcomes.
findings.finding: The plan does not define who retires/removes items from the shared 'queued Autosubmit changes' queue after the batch lands, nor what happens to queued items if the batch fails mid-way. There is no RETIRE path, so the queue can leak stale entries or double-process items on retry.
findings.location: ## What — 'Assemble the queued Autosubmit changes/stacks'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency-safety mechanism (e.g., a distributed lock or exclusive lease acquired before queue read, or an idempotent batch-ID that prevents duplicate CI triggers and submits) to ensure at-most-one active batch run at a time.
findings.finding: The plan does not address concurrency safety on the shared queue: two concurrent or retried batch-runner instances could both read the same queued items, assemble competing chains, and race to trigger CI and submit, with no locking, CAS, or ownership token preventing double-processing.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or add a hard gate) that the `Autosubmit` Gerrit label from Phase 1 is deployed to `infra/gerrit/project.config` before Phase 2 work begins; the plan should name the specific Phase 1 story/ticket that delivers it and make it a blocking dependency.
findings.finding: The plan's core operating premise — 'queued `Autosubmit` changes/stacks' — asserts the `Autosubmit` Gerrit label exists and is already being used to mark changes for auto-landing. A grep of `infra/gerrit/project.config` and all of `infra/gerrit/` returns zero matches for `Autosubmit`. The label does not exist in the repo. Phase 2 batching has no queue to draw from.
findings.location: What section: 'Assemble the queued `Autosubmit` changes/stacks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit blocking dependency line referencing the Phase 1 story that delivers the `Autosubmit` label (e.g., 'Blocked by: S_-label-deployment (843b)') and add a per-AC pre-condition: 'GIVEN the `Autosubmit` label is live in `infra/gerrit/project.config`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/gerrit/gerrit_to_platform.ini.template` and `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (specifically the `vote` job's per-change input schema) as affected/in-scope artifacts, and describe how the single-CI-run AC interacts with the existing per-patchset dispatch mechanism.
findings.finding: AC2 ('Triggers exactly ONE CI run on the combined tip') directly constrains the existing CI dispatch path — gerrit-to-platform (`infra/gerrit/gerrit_to_platform.ini.template`) fires `patchset-created` events and dispatches `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` per patchset. The `vote` job in `gerrit-verify.yaml` then casts `Verified` on a specific `GERRIT_CHANGE_NUMBER`/`GERRIT_PATCHSET_NUMBER`. A single combined-tip trigger would either bypass or reconfigure this per-change dispatch mechanism, yet neither `gerrit_to_platform.ini.template` nor `gerrit-verify.yaml` nor the `Verified` vote-cast job are enumerated as affected edit targets.
findings.location: AC2 — 'Triggers exactly ONE CI run on the combined tip'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to the combined-chain assembly mechanic, verify via a Gerrit API spike (or Gerrit docs/source citation) that: (a) multiple independently-uploaded stacks can be combined into one relation chain, (b) ancestor-atomic submit works for multi-uploader combined chains under FFO, and (c) whether `submitWholeTopic = true` on a single shared topic is the Gerrit-native equivalent — and explicitly evaluate the ADR-0040 parallel-sibling FFO limitation against the proposed pattern.
findings.finding: The plan's core mechanic — assembling queued stacks from independent uploaders into one combined Gerrit relation chain, running one CI pass on the tip, then submitting atomically — is characterized as a 'Graphite/bors stack-aware model on Gerrit relation chains,' implying established prior art. But Graphite and bors operate on GitHub PRs, not Gerrit relation chains. The plan never verifies that Gerrit relation chains from distinct uploaders can be combined into a single chain and submitted ancestor-atomically under FFO; and it does not evaluate whether the already-deployed `submitWholeTopic = true` mechanism (ADR-0040, which explicitly describes the 'Gerrit-native commit queue idiom — the Android/Chromium/Go lineage') is the actual vehicle for this pattern, or whether it has correctness gaps (ADR-0040 explicitly warns that 'parallel sibling changes to `main` cannot be linearized and its atomic submit fails').
findings.location: What section: 'Graphite/bors stack-aware model on Gerrit relation chains'; AC1: 'Assembles queued changes/stacks into one combined linear chain'; AC3: 'submits the whole batch atomically (ancestor-atomic submit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a §Prior Art / Feasibility section that: (1) cites ADR-0040's 'lightweight Gerrit commit-queue bot' pointer as the lineage for this plan; (2) replaces the GitHub-centric 'Graphite/bors' reference with Gerrit-native analogues (Chromium's autosubmit plugin, Android's Gerrit commit queue); (3) includes a one-sentence proof-of-feasibility or API citation for multi-uploader combined-chain submit under FFO; (4) explicitly records whether submitWholeTopic on one shared batch topic is considered and why/why-not.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete batch-size cap in AC4 (e.g., 'maximum N stacks or M total CI jobs per batch, where N/M are the values that keep total GHA job concurrency ≤ 20 for Free tier') so the executor has an actionable default.
findings.finding: AC4 defers the actual batch-size bound to the executor: '~3 concurrent runs' in the Why section is approximate and 'one CI run's job budget' is undefined — no concrete number of stacks, jobs, or changes is stated, so the executor must choose the cap themselves.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4; Why section ('~3 concurrent runs')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'fits the GHA concurrency cap' with a specific number, e.g., 'bounded to ≤ 20 jobs total (Free-tier GHA per-org concurrency limit); the bound is declared as a named constant MAX_BATCH_JOBS in the Autosubmit config and enforced before CI is triggered.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the stack-ordering rule for AC1 (e.g., 'FIFO by Autosubmit queue insertion time, with dependency edges from Phase 1 respected') so the executor can implement assembly without a clarifying question.
findings.finding: AC1's assembly algorithm is unspecified: the plan does not state how competing or conflicting stacks are ordered into the single linear chain, nor what happens when two queued stacks touch the same files.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Stacks are linearized in FIFO order of their Autosubmit queue entry timestamp; stacks with explicit dependency edges (from Phase 1 metadata) are topologically sorted before FIFO ordering is applied within each level.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight empirical probe (e.g., a dry-run with a 2–3 stack batch measuring actual GHA queue time and job-slot consumption) to derive the batch-size bound before coding the limit into the implementation.
findings.finding: The batch-size bound ('fits the GHA concurrency cap') is an asserted heuristic with no measurement or spike planned to validate it; the actual job-budget limit per run and the concurrency cap interaction are assumed, not derived.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Batch size bounded to one CI run's job budget (fits the GHA concurrency cap); documents the bound.'
findings.suggested_fix: Before implementing the bound, run a 2–3 stack synthetic batch through the GHA pipeline and record: (a) actual concurrent-job slots consumed, (b) queue wait time vs. serial baseline. Use the measured values to set and document the bound rather than asserting it from the nominal ~3-runner cap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section capturing the batch-assembly + atomic-land architectural decision, including the rationale for the Graphite/bors relation-chain model and the Phase 2 scope boundary.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel pipeline stage (batch assembler → single CI trigger → atomic land) that constitutes an architectural decision, but no ADR or design-doc update is called out; only the batch-size bound is marked for documentation.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name at least one concrete file path or module location (e.g., `infra/lander/batch_assembler.py` or `infra/scripts/batch-land.sh`) for each new behavioral artifact introduced by Phase 2, or explicitly classify the entire plan as CREATION and acknowledge no existing files require modification.
findings.finding: The plan introduces entirely new behavior — batch chain assembler, single-CI dispatcher, atomic batch lander — but names zero file paths or module locations where this new code will reside. Per G1G2, CREATION of new behavior is out-of-scope for edit-target enumeration unless the plan explicitly names a new artifact location. The plan's Scope section says only 'Phase 2. The batching assembly + single-run + atomic land' with no artifact location.
findings.location: What / Scope sections — 'Assemble the queued Autosubmit changes/stacks... trigger CI ONCE... submits the whole batch atomically'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
